issue_title: Installation fail when your system encoding is cp1251.py
issue_body:
I was trying to install pook on my machine with system preference encoding win-1251 and get exception while installing

```
pip install --upgrade pook
Collecting pook
  Using cached pook-0.1.3.tar.gz
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "C:\Users\sky\AppData\Local\Temp\pip-build-nd_a6pc3\pook\setup.py", line 55, in <module>
        readme = f.read()
      File "C:\Python\Python35-32\lib\encodings\cp1251.py", line 23, in decode
        return codecs.charmap_decode(input,self.errors,decoding_table)[0]
    UnicodeDecodeError: 'charmap' codec can't decode byte 0x98 in position 1842: character maps to <undefined>
```
Problem in reading file `README.rst` in symbol `✘`
Explicit set encoding when calling read function in setup.py to solve this

issue_title: move mad calculation outside of lexicase population loop
issue_body: case statistics should just be calculated once rather than each selection event.

issue_title: WARNING: Failed to send notification using linux
issue_body:
After installing the dependencies, I tried ntfy.

```
root@3b84f399b67d:~# ntfy -b linux -v send 'test'
WARNING: Failed to send notification using linux
root@3b84f399b67d:~# ntfy send test
WARNING: Failed to send notification using linux
root@3b84f399b67d:~# ntfy done sleep 2
WARNING: Failed to send notification using linux
```

Without any other exceptions showing, I can not find out the root cause.

issue_title: Tenacity only works with Tornado 4.5 or above
issue_body:
Hello! I'm running on Tornado 4.4.3, but my tests fail with the following:

```
../app/thunderflock.py:58: in <module>
    wait=wait_fixed(DatabaseRetryConfig['retry_wait_seconds']))
../.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tenacity/__init__.py:95: in wrap
    elif tornado and tornado.gen.is_coroutine_function(f):
E   AttributeError: module 'tornado.gen' has no attribute 'is_coroutine_function'
```

`tornado.gen.is_coroutine_function` was added in Tornado 4.5. Would be great to display this in the README (or add support for Tornado 4.4, ideally!).

issue_title: Garmin (and possibly other proprietary) sentences don't parse correctly
issue_body:
My Garmin GPSMAP 78s outputs the following proprietary sentences:
`$PGRME,27.5,M,46.0,M,53.6,M*1C`
`$PGRMZ,50,f,*1D`
`$PGRMM,WGS 84*06`

These are not correctly parsed by pynmea2 because it puts the sentence subtype ('E', 'Z', or 'M', respectively) as the first data item. Thus I get the following output for the above messages, respectively:
`<GRME(hpe='E', hpe_unit='27.5', vpe='M', vpe_unit='46.0', osepe='M', osepe_unit='53.6') data=['M']>`
`<GRMZ(altitude='Z', altitude_unit='50', pos_fix_dim='f') data=['']>`
`<GRMM(datum='M') data=['WGS 84']>`

issue_title: Vint throwing exception on non-alpha options
issue_body:
Vint is throwing an exception for non-alpha options (i.e. `[a-z]+`), which appear to be valid options in Neovim (e.g. `<F13>`).

For example, the following option (necessary to to get control-shift working in iTerm2.app for me) that overrides the "F13 key" to map hex codes (see this [post](http://www.daniellesucher.com/2014/04/24/my-new-favorite-vim-tmux-bug/)), e.g.
```
set <F13>=^[[25~
map  <F13> <S-CR>
map! <F13> <S-CR>
```
gives:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/vint", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
...
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vint/linting/policy/prohibit_abbreviation_option.py", line 62, in is_valid
    option_name = re.match(r'[a-z]+', option_expr).group(0)
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'group'
```

I was able to work around it by replacing the option in `.vimrc` with
```
set t_F3=^[[25~
map  <F13> <S-CR>
map! <F13> <S-CR>
```

issue_title: Simplifying Units or Removing Redundant Units
issue_body:
Is there a function that will simplify or remove the redundant units.
Example
ureg('in')*ureg('s')/ureg('cm')
    displays the units "in * s / cm"
function_that_simplifies_units(ureg('in')*ureg('s')/ureg('cm'))
    displays the units "s"

I want to express the newtonian constant of gravitation in particular units. I can to that with the following script:

`import pint

ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
ureg.load_definitions('default_en.txt') 

G = 1* ureg.newtonian_constant_of_gravitation

ureg.default_system = 'mysys'
print G.to_base_units()
`

where the file _default_en.txt_ contains:

`
my_distance = 3.085678e21  * centimeter
my_mass     = 1.989e43     * gram 
my_velocity = 1e5	   * centimeter/second
my_time     = my_distance/my_velocity

@system mysys
    my_distance
    my_mass
    my_time
@end
`
In this case, I get the correct answer:

> 43018.9662045 my_distance ** 3 / my_mass / my_time ** 2

I would like now to the same programmatically. For that, I did:


`
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
ureg.define("my_distance = 3.085678e21 * centimeter")
ureg.define("my_mass = 1.989e43 * gram") 
ureg.define("my_velocity = 1e5 * centimeter/second") 
ureg.define("my_time   = my_distance/my_velocity")

issue_title: How is network byte order handled.
issue_body:
_From [rudebo...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/108270286804570581450/) on March 28, 2010 18:18:54_

Hello, im creating an arp broadcast packet like this:

import dpkt
import socket
import binascii


def eth_aton(buffer):
        sp = buffer.split(':')
        buffer = ''.join(sp)
        return binascii.unhexlify(buffer)

arp = dpkt.arp.ARP()
arp.sha=eth_aton('00:24:8c:5b:11:ec')
arp.spa=socket.inet_aton('192.168.1.3')
arp.tha=eth_aton('00:00:00:00:00:00')  
arp.tpa=socket.inet_aton('192.168.1.4')
arp.op=dpkt.arp.ARP_OP_REQUEST  
eth=dpkt.ethernet.Ethernet()
eth.src=arp.sha       
eth.dst=eth_aton('ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff')           
eth.data=arp                      
eth.type=dpkt.ethernet.ETH_TYPE_ARP                      

And Im able to send the packet with a PF_PACKET RAW socket:

s = socket.socket(socket.PF_PACKET, socket.SOCK_RAW) 
s.bind((device, dpkt.ethernet.ETH_TYPE_ARP))
s.send(str(eth))            

Everyhting works great and Im able to get a reply, my question is How is
the network byte order handled? dpkt or the BSD socket handles it? I've
tried it in systems with different endiannesses and it works fine.

issue_title: Output file validation breaks when using temp files
issue_body:
Output file validation, and in particular [this line](https://github.com/rabitt/pysox/blob/master/sox/file_info.py#L261), crashes when trying to write to a temp file (created using `tempfile`). Not sure which version this was introduced in, since I didn't use to have this problem.

Minimal example:
```python
import os, tempfile
 tf = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix='.wav', delete=True)
os.path.dirname(tf)
```

Possible fixes: ensure output file validation supports tempfiles, or at least allow to disable output file validation.

# Preparation for a more verbose output

issue_title: Should not require pytest-runner to install the library
issue_body:
Listing pytest-runner in the setup_requires metadata means that in order to install keyring, even in production, the test runner is needed. There's no reason to do that, since if pytest-runner is installed it can run the tests just fine without being in setup_requires.

Having this installation dependency is currently breaking the automated tests in several OpenStack projects which are trying to use keyring.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/106
- Originally reported by: Doug Hellmann
- Originally created at: 2013-07-21T18:45:05.463

issue_title: error with @autoclass concerning default arguments' type
issue_body:
I would like to provide an example showing how pytypes can be used with autoclass.
However the following code fails with a strange error:

```python
from autoclass import autoclass, Boolean
from pytypes import typechecked
from numbers import Real, Integral
from typing import Optional

@typechecked
@autoclass
class HouseConfiguration(object):
    def __init__(self,
                 name: str,
                 surface: Real,
                 nb_floors: Optional[Integral] = 1,
                 with_windows: Boolean = False):
        pass

    # -- overriden setter for surface for custom validation
    @setter_override
    def surface(self, surface):
        assert surface > 0
        self._surface = surface

t = HouseConfiguration('test', 12, 2)  # error
```

The error received is : 

```bash
Expected: Tuple[str, Real, Union[Integral, NoneType], Boolean]
Received: Tuple[str, int, int, int]
```

While this works:
```python
t = HouseConfiguration('test', 12, nb_floors=2)
```

So it seems that this does not have anything to do with autoclass: somehow positional arguments / default values in the constructor signature are not handled properly.

issue_title: Fixing use of explicit type comparison to use of isinstance should not be performed within __eq__
issue_body:
When using `-a`, `type(a) == type(b)` is fixed to `isinstance(a, type(b))`.

Within a `__eq__` method, this pattern should be allowed:
```
class A(object):
    ...

    def __eq__(self, other):
        if type(self) != type(other):
            return False
        ...
```

In the example above, the replacement adds undesired behavior.

issue_title: Handle single and multiple loads of same record
issue_body: If the same record is loaded both singly (e.g. using `one` or `first`) and multiply, we can get false positives for lazy loads. To fix, we can ignore lazy loads on records that were loaded singly. Note that this logic could lead to false negatives, which isn't ideal--but it's less confusing than the status quo. See https://github.com/flyerhzm/bullet/blob/master/lib/bullet/active_record5.rb#L53-L56 for an example.

issue_title: Improved sample for Use in README.md
issue_body:
Thanks for these interfaces, it's working well for me.

Your starting sample in the Use section of the README.md was not updating the tracking numbers for me, just repeating the same numbers 100 times.

This might be a Vive/Rift difference.  My test case here is a CV1 Rift.  Python 3.5.

I tweaked the code by adding the creation of the Compositor object, and then use waitGetPoses, as it seems required to update the head tracking positions.

Here is the sample with the two added lines. While running in my test case, it updates the tracking numbers.

Please feel free to use this or not as you prefer.

````
import sys
import time
import openvr

vr_system = openvr.init(openvr.VRApplication_Scene)
vr_compositor = openvr.IVRCompositor()

for i in range(100):
    poses = vr_system.getDeviceToAbsoluteTrackingPose(
        openvr.TrackingUniverseStanding,
        0,
        openvr.k_unMaxTrackedDeviceCount)
    pose = poses[openvr.k_unTrackedDeviceIndex_Hmd]
    vr_compositor.waitGetPoses(poses, openvr.k_unMaxTrackedDeviceCount, None, 0)  
    print(pose.mDeviceToAbsoluteTracking)
    sys.stdout.flush()
    time.sleep(0.2)

openvr.shutdown
````

issue_title: Creat temp file inside a subdirectory under /tmp/
issue_body:
Hi,

It would be really good if the all temporary file pathes aggreagted into a subdirectory of `/tmp/ e.g. `/tmp/pybedtool/` (same as practice done by many tools like Mozilla). Having this reduces the side-effects of pybedtools tmp files on the OS and other programs, specially in the scenarios where many tmp files are created. Currently I am facing the problem where too many tmp files, makes the other programs slow, probably because they need to list the content of /tmp/ main directory which is overwhelmed with the pybedtools temp files.

issue_title: timeout for queries in spec files
issue_body:
If due to a performance regression or bug a query suddenly is a lot slower and takes hours instead of seconds it kills the whole benchmark suite and makes running the same specs across different versions troublesome.

So it would be great to be able to define a timeout per query so that it will be interrupted & skipped if it takes longer to execute.

Issues Handling Parse of iso8601 datetime strings
issue_body:
```
GOOD (precise offset): '2015-02-04T16:33:21.247513-05:00'
GOOD (precise utc indication): '2013-09-30T15:34:00.000Z'

Delorean(datetime=2015-02-04 16:33:21.247513+00:00, timezone=UTC)

(True,Delorean(datetime=2015-02-04 16:33:21.247513+00:00, timezone=UTC))

issue_title: Producers don't fail over when a broker crashes abruptly
issue_body:
We're pushing about 1,000 events per second to Kafka from a cluster of 50+ servers. When a kafka broker is terminated abruptly, about 10% of the clients do not seem to fail over to the backup node to continue the writes. The throughput drops to about 900 events per second, and we see a lot of `KafkaTimeoutError` errors. We're on version `1.3.1` and are using the sync interface like this:

``` python
try:
    future = self.kafka_producer.send(topic, message)
    future.get(timeout=0.5)
except KafkaError as e:
    raise e        
```

Here are the other non-default client settings we use:

```
metadata_max_age_ms = 20000
request_timeout_ms = 500
reconnect_backoff_ms = 1000
```

These clients do not recover even after 10 minutes. If I did a graceful shutdown of the Kafka broker, 
there is absolutely no issue - failover happens promptly without any disruption. I ran this experiment multiple times over multiple days to verify.

issue_title: Move Oauth refresh token out of the config file
issue_body:
Currently the oauth token is stored in the rtv config file as
```
[oauth]
acccess_token=...
refresh_token=...
```
I was thinking that instead of doing this, we could keep a separate key file in the same directory. This would be cleaner in situations where the user doesn't have a config file, or their config file is read-only. Something like

**rtv.oauth** or **oauth.txt**
```
oauth_refresh_token=refresh_token.
```

Could even get fancy and store tokens for multiple users in the same file, e.g.
```
user_1=refresh_token...
user_2=refresh_token...
user_3=refresh_token...

issue_title: Unhandled exception when redirect without Location header is scanned
issue_body:
An unhandled exception occurs when a server responds with a redirect, but the first response packet does not contain a "Location" HTTP header.  This happens with any of the standard scanner plugins.

Output:
Unhandled exception when processing --tlsv1:
TypeError - cannot concatenate 'str' and 'NoneType' objects

Reason in /utils/ssl_connection.py:
```python
if 300 <= http_response.status < 400:
    # Add redirection URL to the result
    redirect = ' - ' + http_response.getheader('Location', None)
```

If a redirect response code is received, but there isn't a Location header in the first packet, then this particular getheader() call returns None, leading to the exception.

Recommended fix:
```python
if 300 <= http_response.status < 400:
    if http_response.getheader('Location', None):
        # Add redirection URL to the result
        redirect = ' - ' + http_response.getheader('Location', None)
```

I'll submit a pull request.

issue_title: Add proxy command capabilities to SshShell
issue_body:
Hello,

This is sort a feature request / proposition.

When using paramiko's client.connect, you can can specify a sock=,  this parameter can be passed an instance of paramiko.proxy.ProxyCommand instanciated with a proxy command such as...
"ssh -q -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null user@proxyname nc -q0 1.2.3.4 22"
Which would connect to 1.2.3.4 through the given proxy

So i would like to propose this change but wanted to know how you would see it implemented:

* Ultra simple but very unituitive: spur.SshShell() takes a sock= param, keep it in self and pass it co client.connect in _connect_ssh 
  * you would have to craft your proxy command yourself
  * could be useful for other use cases

* Simple and more user-friendly : spur.SshShell() takes a proxy_command= param, keep it in self and makes a ProxyCommand to pass to client.connect in _connect_ssh 
  * User needs understanding of proxy command (an example would be supplied) but won't have to directly refer to paramiko

* More complex but most user-friendly i think:  spur.SshShell() takes a proxy **object** that would define a user / pass / key for the proxy along with the port and some options, one would say you could even supply a SshShell as a proxy (value wise... maybe not implementation wise)
  * Could look like SshShell(username... proxy=SshShell(username...))
  * Or something along the lines of proxy=Proxy(username...) which would be a new object related only to proxying values.


What do you think?

issue_title: What's the benefit for aiohttp-wsgi + django?
issue_body:
It seems that `aiohttp-wsgi` just runs an application in an executor, which by default [is just a thread pool](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloop.html#executor), with a default pool size of 5 threads.

So if the application is designed with blocking APIs, such as flask and django, it will actually be executed via multi-threading and could not take advantage of co-routines. Then what's the benefit for using `aiohttp-wsgi`?

The implementation for [`get_table_names`](https://github.com/kennethreitz/records/blob/133532bdc300e551c2aeecf4423df7fab9b9e4fc/records.py#L224) is odd.

The following steps are executed every time, making the function much more expensive than it needs to be:

1. a new engine is created rather than using the existing one to check out a connection
2. a declarative base is created just to use its metadata, which is better created using `sqlalchemy.MetaData()`
3. The whole database schema is reflected just to get the table names.

The fix would look something like:

```
from sqlalchemy import inspect

def __init__(...):
    engine = create_engine(...)
    self.db = engine.connect()
    self._engine = engine

def get_table_names():
    return inspect(self._engine).get_table_names()
```

The name `context` is confusing. It is actually the ChatBot instance.

**Details**
When any adapter (input, output, logic, storage) is added to an instance of ChatterBot, it is initialized with a variable called `context` which allows the adapter to access other adapters and variables that were set on the ChatBot instance.

1. The `context` variable should be renamed to `chatterbot` to be more self-explanatory
2. A property should be added that aliases the new `chatterbot` variable and triggers a deprecation warning explaining how to correct the issue.

issue_title: Improve security against maliciously constructed data
issue_body:
**Problem**
`xmltodict` is Expat-based, which is not secure against maliciously constructed data (it's even mentioned on the package docs: https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/pyexpat.html). 

**Possible Solution**
There is a project that improves Expat security: `defusedexpat` (PyPI: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/defusedexpat/ Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/tiran/defusedexpat). Using it instead of regular Expat could resolve security issues.

**Potential problems with implementation**
At this point I haven't researched compatibility issues that may arise if `xmltodict` were to use `defusedexpat`. Will do this in the next few days.

**More information**
https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/xml.html
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/defusedxml/
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/defusedexpat/
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Billion_laughs
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/magazine/ee335713.aspx

Let me know if there's interest in solving this problem. Is somebody interested in forking the repo and implementing the solution or should I do this (I won't be able to work on this until next week)?

2- Users path may be messed up, but being able to specify e.g. `/usr/bin/python36 -m wormhole` removes ambiguity.  For example, if you are currently inside a virtualenv, or doing a `docker exec -it bash`.

issue_title: Headers in Response should be case insensitives
issue_body:
From [RFC 2616 - "Hypertext Transfer Protocol -- HTTP/1.1", Section 4.2, "Message Headers"](http://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec4.html#sec4.2) and [RFC 7230](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-3.2):

> Each header field consists of a **case-insensitive field name** followed
   by a colon (":"), optional leading whitespace, the field value, and
   optional trailing whitespace.

Following this statement, can you make the required change in the Response object ? (you could base that change on https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/structures.py#L15 for example)

**Reason behind this change**
HTTP Servers (nginx, apache, whatever) have to follow these RFC, that means they can send you headers capitalized (`Content-Type` for example), but also without upper case at all (`content-type`)

In this case, when you work with headers, adding capitalized version in your check, if the server once send the lower case version, it'll fail because `Response` object is case sensitive for headers

Example:
```
res = client.request(some_operation())

# here we check header for the "Expires" header to manage caching
# but if the server returns "expires", as it could happen, we won't do anything
if res.header['Expires']:
    # do some caching stuff
```

Thanks !

I think it would be better to show label of the cell in the cell repr instead or row and column number.
Currently it shows as `R1C1` change this to `A1`.

issue_title: Fix waits/locks to cope with uninterruptible locking
issue_body:
I've recently discovered that some versions of Python (at least 2.7 - seems fixed in 3.6) are entirely uninterruptible when waiting on locks with no "timeout" parameter, i.e. it'll retry on every EINTR from sem_wait with no way to customize that behavior.

This could mean shutting down an executor can hang indefinitely and may not be interruptible by SIGTERM/SIGINT.  To minimize problems relating to this, I think any possible calls to a wait/join/etc from the main thread should be reviewed and possibly given a timeout parameter, which makes it interruptible.

issue_title: If using match_hostname, subject_alt_name support should be mandatory
issue_body:
A good reason to use backports.ssl is that it makes correct SNI and match_hostname support available to python programs running on RHEL 7 or CentOS 7.  These OSes ship with python 2.7.5, and this support was only added into mainstream python in 2.7.9.

However, it turns out that match_hostname silently fails to decode subject_alt_name extension records if the subject_alt_name import fails:

```
try:
    from .subject_alt_name import get_subject_alt_name
except ImportError:
    get_subject_alt_name = None
```

This can happen, for example, if the pyasn1 library (python-pyasn1 RPM) isn't installed.

The net effect is that match_hostname erroneously fails, e.g. against domains such as pypi.python.org, which is listed as a SAN.

My RFE is that if match_hostname is being used, it should fail as early as possible if subject_alt_name isn't available.

Here's a test case:

```
import sys
import socket

if sys.version_info >= (2, 7, 9):
    import ssl
else:
    import backports.ssl as ssl


(OP_NO_SSLv2, OP_NO_SSLv3, OP_NO_COMPRESSION, OP_CIPHER_SERVER_PREFERENCE,
 OP_SINGLE_DH_USE, OP_SINGLE_ECDH_USE) = (16777216, 33554432, 131072, 4194304,
                                          1048576, 524288)

def mk_clientctx():
    ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv23)
    ctx.options |= OP_NO_SSLv2 | OP_NO_SSLv3 | OP_NO_COMPRESSION
    ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED
    ctx.check_hostname = True
    ctx.load_verify_locations("/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt")
    return ctx

d = "pypi.python.org"
s = socket.socket()
s.connect((d, 443))
clientctx = mk_clientctx()
s = clientctx.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=d)
```

On CentOS 7 / RHEL 7, if python-pyasn1 is available, this test case works fine.  If not, you get the error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 25, in <module>
    s = clientctx.wrap_socket(s, server_hostname=d)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/backports/ssl/core.py", line 669, in wrap_socket
    self.check_hostname)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/backports/ssl/core.py", line 241, in __init__
    self.do_handshake()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/backports/ssl/core.py", line 263, in do_handshake
    match_hostname(self.getpeercert(), self._conn.get_servername().decode('utf-8'))
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/backports/ssl/core.py", line 184, in match_hostname
    % (hostname, dnsnames[0]))
backports.ssl.core.CertificateError: hostname u'pypi.python.org' doesn't match u'www.python.org'
```

In https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/master/social/storage/base.py,  the `CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX` maybe error.  

```
CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX = re.compile(r'[^\w.@+-_]+', re.UNICODE)
```

The `-` in `[]` means range of characters, if we want to match literal `-`, we can escape it or put it in the end.

The correct expression is

```
CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX = re.compile(r'[^\w.@+_-]+', re.UNICODE)
```

or

`CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX = re.compile(r'[^\w.@+\-_]+', re.UNICODE)`

ref: https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/re.html#regular-expression-syntax

issue_title: current_user is set to False after long running sessions
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

I'm having an odd intermittent issue with I think specifically the request context, and the value of the `current_user` object. I'll try my best to state it all as clearly as possible

#### Pre-conditions:

1. having already successfully logged on earlier and a session cookie is set and present
2. some long-ish time has elapsed with the python server running still
3. return to the browser and try to navigate directly to a view that is protected with `@login_required`
4. `AttributeError` error appears

#### Environment details:

1. Flask 0.10.1
2. Flask-login 0.3.2
3. Flask development server
4. browser chrome

#### The problem
The error I get is an `AttributeErrorr: 'bool' object has no attribute 'is_authenticated'`. I think odd, because there should be no bool values anywhere near the user_object. So I traced back through the debug output and discovered that `current_user` was set to False. I have created a gist here: https://gist.github.com/HUSSTECH/92ffadf11c938f14847f47d8690d9fad

In the gist I have the full traceback, an expanded view of the code that raised the error in the decorator (line 7), and some outputs from the debug console from within the request context showing the False values. So the big mystery for me is how the user_object was ever set to False. I see nothing in the flask-login code that could have caused it to be False (the documents are very clear about returning `None` and not `False`!). Also in my code, I don't set it to False anywhere. It's doubly strange as the traceback does not even enter my app code, it goes from the request handler to the view handler, where it is intercepted by `@login_required` and then we get the error. I've confirmed none of my code is called, it never steps into the `user_loader` or anything like that.

#### The fix?
The two ways that I stop the problem are one of:

1. navigate to the login view on the browser, re-authenticate myself and I think that "cleans" things up and it all works again...or
2. delete the session cookie from my browser and refresh the exact same page that has the error and I'm redirected to the login view automatically, and again all is good.

In my mind, I'm drawn towards asking the questions, how does `current_user` get set to False? If I'm reading the code right, it's set from the `_request_ctx_stack.top.user`, so how is that False? and why does deleting the session stop the error?

Sorry for the obscure and long description, I've searched this repo and the internet with no luck on this, so hopefully it may be of use to others too. Big thanks for the library, and thanks for any light you may be able to shed here! :smile:

issue_title: Considering using groups and <use ...> ID references
issue_body:
This project is awesome, but one thing you may want to consider is using `<g>` groups in a larger hidden SVG tag and then outputting `<svg ...><use xlink:href="#id"></svg>` where the SVG should go. As it stands right now it looks like you're outputting the full SVG each time, which ultimately increases the total size on the request. By using the groups you can just shove each SVG in once and go from there - this works for me in my personal projects, and I've not found any browser issues with it (someone can correct me if I'm wrong).

I'm not sure why but I don't see people do this too often, seems overlooked in terms of a good practice. Maybe a configurable option?

/api/things/{group}/
/api/things/{group}/paged/{page}/

-        apis = urlparser.get_apis(urlconf=urlconf, filter_path=filter_path)

issue_title: Key error 'id' when doing PUT request
issue_body:
[DESCRIPTION]
When making a PUT request to a `BulkModelViewSet`, using a simple DRF `ModelSerializer` with all fields automatically generated - no field specified by hand -and with `list_serializer_class` attribute being `BulkListSerializer` the application crashes at [this line](https://github.com/miki725/django-rest-framework-bulk/blob/2a59d14e7036660f00996b013ab913678b5974c4/rest_framework_bulk/drf3/serializers.py#L42) and raises a `KeyError` for `'id'` key.

[REASON]
When [DRF `ListSerializer` passes `validated_data` to the `update()` method](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/serializers.py#L654)  the given `validated_data` don't contain the id of the object.

[FIX]
DRF don't return `id` in `validated_data` anymore unless the field is explicitly declared in the serializer (`id = serializers.IntegerField()`) so you have to do that in order for it to work. I don't believe we should add it to DRF-bulk `BulkListSerializer` though, since `pk` might not be the `id` field, what do you think?

[ENVIRONMENT]

The current link to the library documentation in the README.rst is set to `http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.org`.  This resolves to `http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.org/index.html` which is actually our index module documentation. We may want to consider either changing the link to specifically point to `http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.org/default.html` or renaming index.html to something different and renaming default.html to index.html.

issue_title: Defining Residual Convolution Structures in Block from gluon Module
issue_body: Please see this [S.O. post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/46306782/mxnet-python3-defining-a-residual-convolution-structures-as-block-from-gluon-m) and if possible answer all questions / subquestions. If you prefer, the content can be copy-pasted here.

## Error Message:
conv_out should be multiplied by the average of the conv_out_grad in the reference paper. but conv_out be multiplied by the average of the imggrad in your gradcam.py.

## What have you tried to solve it?

def get_cam(conv_out_grad, conv_out):
    """Compute CAM. Refer section 3 of https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.02391 for details"""
    weights = np.mean(conv_out_grad, axis=(1, 2))
    cam = np.ones(conv_out.shape[1:], dtype=np.float32)
    for i, w in enumerate(weights):
        cam += w * conv_out[i, :, :]
    cam = np.maximum(cam, 0)
    cam = (cam - np.min(cam)) / (np.max(cam) - np.min(cam)) 
    cam = np.uint8(cam * 255)
    return cam

def visualize(net, preprocessed_img, orig_img, conv_layer_name):
    # Returns grad-cam heatmap, guided grad-cam, guided grad-cam saliency
    imggrad = get_image_grad(net, preprocessed_img)
    conv_out, conv_out_grad = get_conv_out_grad(net, preprocessed_img, conv_layer_name=conv_layer_name)

    cam = get_cam(conv_out_grad, conv_out)
    cam = cv2.resize(cam, (imggrad.shape[1], imggrad.shape[2]))
    
    ggcam = get_guided_grad_cam(cam, imggrad)
    img_ggcam = grad_to_image(ggcam)
    
    img_heatmap = get_img_heatmap(orig_img, cam)
    
    ggcam_gray = to_grayscale(ggcam)
    img_ggcam_gray = np.squeeze(grad_to_image(ggcam_gray))
    
    return img_heatmap, img_ggcam, img_ggcam_gray

issue_title: Half-precision support for optimizers other than SGD
issue_body:
I would like to have some parameters of a network to be half-precision to save memory.
I am running into inconsistent behavior of different optimizers when using half-precision (float16) variables: for a toy network of y = softmax(w*x) with x float32 and w float16 cast to float32, the Adam optimizer sometimes(!) produces nan weights/scores, whereas SGD seems to work fine.
(Run example below with x_dtype = 'float32' and w_dtype = 'float16').
Other optimizers such as adagrad/adadelta don't seem to support float16 at all, leading to a attr check fail.

## Environment info
Operating System:
MacOS

MXNet version:
8b6c8598f8f15a765dd9695c92ce251cd9eda747

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import mxnet as mx
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

x_dtype='float32'
w_dtype='float16'
optimizer = 'adam'

x = mx.sym.Variable('data', dtype=x_dtype)
label = mx.sym.Variable('softmax_label', dtype=x_dtype)
w = mx.sym.cast(mx.sym.Variable("weight", dtype=w_dtype), dtype=x_dtype)
y = mx.sym.FullyConnected(x, w, num_hidden=10)
y = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=y, label=label)
mod = mx.module.Module(y)
it = mx.io.NDArrayIter(mx.nd.uniform(0,1,(10,10), dtype=x_dtype), mx.nd.ones((10,), dtype=x_dtype))
mod.fit(it, num_epoch=10, eval_metric='ce', optimizer=optimizer, optimizer_params={'learning_rate': 0.00001})
```

x_dtype | w_dtype | optimizer | outcome
------------ | ------------- | ------------- | -------------
float32 | float16 | adam | nan (sometimes)
float32 | float16 | adagrad/adadelta | check fail
float32 | float32 | * | ok
float16 | float16 | * | FC float16 only supported by CuDNN

What is the current support of optimizers for these scenarios? If not supported at all, is Adam simply missing a check like AdaGrad/AdaDelta?
At the moment using django `get_or_create` or `update_or_create` doesn't work as some values might be encrypted/hashed. We can have a mixin which would override those methods from django.

Hey there, 
I'm getting test failures when using Django-extensions 1.6.0. The only thing we use from Django-extensions is the JSONField, and using 1.6.0 over 1.5.9 gets some strange JSON errors: `ValueError: Expecting property name enclosed in double quotes: line 1 column 2 (char 1)`.

I've done some digging and it seems that it's trying to decode the str() representation of a dict, rather than the JSON representation, so I'm guessing there is some issue with serializing dictionaries into the JSONField where only str(), not json.dumps(), is being called.

Separating plone.app.event from plone.app.contenttypes did make sense: until 2.0 it contained Archetypes based Event type and Dexterity based behaviors, and this package is older than plone.app.contenttypes.

Since Archetypes support was removed with 2.0 we could merge plone.app.event into plone.app.contenttypes and simplify Plones dependency hell.

By the way, the event accessor pattern can also be removed, because that was only to support Archetypes and Dexterity with the same codebase.
And plone.event should be renamed to something not assuming to deal with an event notification system. Maybe plone.calendaring would be a better name.

Refer to this discussion: https://github.com/plone/Products.CMFPlone/issues/570

issue_title: Is batch size at all related to model geometry?
issue_body:
I've been running TED on 1080 Ti for a while now (it's around 18 hours in) and am now concerned that with the default batch sizes (16, 8, 8) I _may_ run into OOM issues.

Please could someone confirm if stopping a run and changing the batch size has any impact on the model geometry (_sorry, it's probably obvious to others, but I couldn't infer it from docs or looking over  issues_).

The reason I ask is that there is this point in [README.md](/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/README.md): 
> Be aware however that checkpoints are only valid for the same model geometry they had been generated from. In other words: If there are error messages of certain Tensors having incompatible dimensions, this is most likely due to an incompatible model change. One usual way out would be to wipe all checkpoint files in the checkpoint directory or changing it before starting the training.

And if changing the batch size changes the geometry then I  won't be able to continue later with the checkpoint from now.  I may well be out of options, but would rather avoid wasting that ~18 hrs if at all possible!!

Thank you
Neil
As a result of the merge, commit 84c030a, the ability to overfit the TED data disappeared. It appears as if some bug was introduced in the merge.

issue_title: ExpiresIn not effective in boto3 s3 generate_presigned_url
issue_body:
python 2.7.6 on bo3o 1.1.14
The generate_presigned_url function on s3 client seems to store a value of the first "ExpiresIn" parameter that is passed in, and uses that from that point onward.

```
>>> import boto3
>>> s3_connection = boto3.client('s3')
>>> s3_connection.generate_presigned_url("get_object", Params={'Bucket': 'foo', 'Key': 'bar.txt'}, ExpiresIn=100)
u'https://s3.amazonaws.com/foo/internal/customers/bar.txt?X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Expires=100&X-Amz-Credential=...
>>> s3_connection.generate_presigned_url("get_object", Params={'Bucket': 'foo', 'Key': 'bar.txt'}, ExpiresIn=200)
u'https://s3.amazonaws.com/syapse-fstore-us-west-1/internal/customers/localhost8000/test/FileRec/2011/8/another.txt?X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Expires=100&X-Amz-Credential=...
```

Note that the first value of ExpiresIn is propagated throughout future calls into the X-Amz-Expires value.

issue_title: Should Repo.tags return tags sorted by date, not alphabetical?
issue_body:
At present, `git.Repo.tags` returns the list of tags sorted alphabetically. It might be more helpful to have the list of tags sorted by the `committed_date` of the relevant `Commit`.

For example, I have a repository with the following tags:

```python
>>> import git
>>> repo = git.Repo('.')
>>> print(repo.tags)
[<git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a1">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a10">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a2">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a3">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a4">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a5">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a6">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a7">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a8">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a9">]
```

Here the ordering is misleading, since the v0.1a10 tag is the most recent. Does it make sense to sort this list before returning?

```python
>>> print(sorted(repo.tags, key=lambda t: t.commit.committed_date))
[<git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a1">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a2">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a3">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a4">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a5">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a6">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a7">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a8">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a9">,
 <git.TagReference "refs/tags/v0.1a10">]

This may also be helpful for other reference lists.

I'm creating my first app with djangocms-blog and might be missing something, but I'm finding the pagination settings strange. Here is why.

Number of posts per page on the main post listing is configured by the app instance. That is perfectly fine. But the other post listings (category, tag, archive, etc.) should follow the same principle. Currently, thet are only configured via `settings.py`.

It seems to me that usually the designs of the main listing and other listings are more-or-less similar, so perhaps they should use the same config (i.e. the app instance). Or alternatively, perhaps we should add another config field to the app instance, so that there are two pagination numbers:

 * posts per page on main page
 * posts per page on other listings (category, post, archive, etc.)

We can obviously also have a separate field for each listing. Would that be an overkill?

I can prepare a pull request if this is something that would be accepted. If so, which option?

1. Pipenv version: 9.0.1

##### Steps to replicate

I'm compiling a private package against a private (system-wide installed) library, so cannot really.

As part of an effort to speed up the locking process, I think we can make some incremental gains by storing a local cache of the dependency graph of each package we resolve for each abi tag + python version.

Essentially I would suggest:

* Flat cache storing 1 package with only one level of nesting
* Each package is a dict-like of containing the name of the package and an iterable of resolved versions
    - Each resolved version stores the following:
        - Hashes
        - ABI tag resolved against (platform/python version/compile flags)
        - dependencies -> dict-like with keys `['name', 'resolved_version', 'specifiers', 'markers']`
* Some implementation of this which could be stored in the lockfile itself to make it portable & avoid re-resolving known graphs

This saves us having to continuously hit pypi for information about things that are not going to change, and allows us to re-resolve and re-lock without re-calculating each setup file over and over again which is incredibly slow.  While this may come at the risk of issues of indeterminism in `setup.py` execution, I think the risk is minimal and the gains are vast. 

Reference issues: #1785 #1866 

/cc @ncoghlan @uranusjr @jtratner

issue_title: Trailing new line symbols in literal notation
issue_body:
Zero or more trailing new line symbols in literal notation in yml are converted to exactly one trailing new line symbol in json. So, having this yml file (indent spaces are visualized with middle dot symbol for readability):
```yml
LINEBREAK_AT_THE_END: |
··Linebreak at the end
··
TWO_LINEBREAKS_AT_THE_END: |
··Two linebreaks at the end
··
··
NO_LINEBREAKS: |
··No linebreaks
ZERO: Zero
```
you will have json like this:
```json
{
  "LINEBREAK_AT_THE_END": "Linebreak at the end\n",
  "TWO_LINEBREAKS_AT_THE_END": "Two linebreaks at the end\n",
  "NO_LINEBREAKS": "No linebreaks\n",
  "ZERO": "Zero"
}
```
Any suggestions on how to deal with this at least with some workaround?

issue_title: Possibility to ignore other warnings in ShouldWarn
issue_body:
I use `ShouldWarn` from time to time, but I found it very difficult to use in cases, when I want to check only one of several warnings that happen to occur.

Consider case:
```python
def deprecated_utility():
    warnings.warn("This utility is deprecated.", DeprecationWarning)

def deprecated_function():
    warnings.warn("This function is deprecated.", DeprecationWarning)
    deprecated_utilitiy()

with ShouldWarn(DeprecationWarning("This function is deprecated.")):
    deprecated_function()
```
This fails because there are two warnings raised instead of only one. I really don't care about the other one, but I only want to check the one I defined. I can bypass that by capturing, but it cumbersome and may be potentially difficult, since warnings may be dependent on the version of python, third party library etc.

I'd like a simple switch, which would ignore all other warnings accidentally captured by `ShouldWarn`.

issue_title: Add a docker config for developpers
issue_body:
What?
------

In order to ease the integration of newcomers, we should have a fully working docker ready to develop.

The idea is to copy what have been done in https://github.com/cernopendata/opendata.cern.ch/blob/master/docker-compose-dev.yml with a specific docker-compose for developpers, with the necessary documentation.

This docker should:
- [x] run a demo site very easily (so it will be very close to the current `docker-compose.yml`
- [x] run invenio in debug mode (auto refresh...)
- [x] mount a dev folder so we can `pip install -e` any modules we want inside the containers, and thus work on a given module
- [ ] integrate a celery worker
- [x] install the debug toolbar
- [ ] add files for records
    - [ ] integrate invenio-records-files
    - [ ] add some example files to the demo records

Any other suggestions?
see https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3803

How?
-----

The idea is to have the following development folder structure:

```yml
- dev
    - invenio (a clone of this repo)
    - modules (folder mounted into the containers)
        - invenio-records
        - invenio-i-like-you
        - ...
    - zenodo
    - ...
```

We have a `dev` folder containing a clone of `invenio`, `zenodo`, or all invenio instances you are working on...

It also contains a specific `modules` folder (you will be able to change the name in the `docker-compose-dev.yml`) that will be mounted in the containers. Thus, you can put here whatever you want to be accessible inside the containers.

Generally, you will have the clones of the modules you are working on here (or symlinks to them, or git-new-workdir, or whatever you want)

Then, you can `pip install -Ue` inside your docker to work on the modules you want.

Originally on 2012-04-10

[Open Archives Initiative Object Reuse and Exchange](http://www.openarchives.org/ore/) defines standards for the description and exchange of aggregations of Web resources [...]

In Invenio aggregation might come from different sources:
* the DB:
  - *collections* aggregate *records*
  - *records* links to *documents*
  - *documents* links to *revisions* of documents
  - *revisions* of document link to *formats* of documents
* [MARC 76x-78x fields](http://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/bd76x78x.html) (at CERN e.g. these are used to link a record to the official publication, or a conference with its talks, a talk with the contribution and the proceeding, etc. or can be used to link a photo to a photoshoot). (in OpenAIREplus datasets will be linked to publications).

Implementation details will be added as comments to this ticket.

issue_title: WebSubmit: improve mail sent by Send_Modify_Mail
issue_body:
Originally on 2010-08-06

The email sent by `Send_Modify_Mail` include the list of fields modified by the user. For eg:

```
Hello:
        Dear Sir or Madam, 
photos DEMO-PICTURE-EXP-2010-001 has just been modified.
Modified fields: DEMOPIC_TITLE DEMOPIC_ABSTRACT Upload_Photos
[...]
```

This list is customizable, but usually refers to the file created by the submission for the function `Create_Modify_Interface.py`, which contains the list of field name codes.

The email can be improved by trying to match the element names to their description retrieved from table 'sbmFIELD', column 'sdesc'.

issue_title: Add roman as a package on conda-forge
issue_body:
While trying to put our package on [conda-forge](https://conda-forge.org/), we ran into a problem since we have roman as a dependency and roman is not on conda-forge.  It would be helpful to the conda-forge community if roman were made available on conda-forge as well, especially since I don't know of any similar packages on conda-forge already.  

In the meantime, our workaround will likely be putting `roman.py` and the license into an `extern` directory inside of our package (in case anyone else runs into this issue).

Thank you for making this package available.  It has been really helpful!

issue_title: add a public api for tracking attempts
issue_body:
The `manager.run(attempts)` api is awesome.  I'd like to use it in pyramid_tm which currently re-implements most of the logic itself. There's one feature that would be really useful which is a public api on the transaction (or the manager) to expose what's happening to underlying code.

Specifically we have some cases where we'd like to do things differently on the last attempt than on earlier attempts that raise retryable exceptions and there's no way to know that we're on the last attempt using the current `run` api.

I'd propose something like `txn.is_last_attempt` or `txn.attempt_index` combined with `txn.max_attempts` or `txn.attempts_remaining`. This would allow underlying code to make decisions on what it does with retryable exceptions.

I'm happy to work on a PR for this but wanted to get some input on the API first. Personally I prefer `txn.is_last_attempt` as I don't have an explicit need to expose the exact number of attempts to underlying code.

issue_title: FileNotFoundError at end of build when copying APK file.
issue_body:
Using buildozer VM and Python 3. Running a simple test app. APK seems to build and work if copied manually to the phone, but deploy does not work.

DEBUG log: https://pastebin.com/raw/zPnkkUTm
buildozer.spec: https://pastebin.com/raw/VdxTZkFm

Hi everyone,
is it already somehow possible to access WiFi information of available networks (SSID, MAC, quality/signal strength)?
There's no recipe for `access_points` so far but maybe you know another way e.g. calling system services?
Thanks a lot!

issue_title: Yahoo finance stock pull missing days
issue_body:
I'm getting a dataframe with missing days scattered somewhat randomly in between. So if I run the following code, I get the output attached file (zipped for upload). In this file, there are dates that are missing in no particular order. 

Am I doing something wrong? Or is there a limit to the amount of data I can pull? If so, what's the logic for missing dates. Thanks in advance for all the help!

[testOutput.csv.zip](https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/files/1577999/testOutput.csv.zip)
```import pandas_datareader.data as data
import datetime

symbol = 'AMZN'
start = datetime.datetime(1980,1,1)
end = datetime.datetime(2017,10,10)	
df = data.DataReader(symbol,'yahoo',start,end)
df.to_csv('testOutput.csv')`

issue_title: Calculate distances in bulk and using numpy.
issue_body: I was recently using Geopy to calculate distances of several hundred thousand lat longs from a single point. It was agonizingly slow - at least in the tens of minutes, if not hour+. Using an open haversine function in which I swapped out the default python math library for numpy and patched to allow for array processing, I got a speed up to ~ 1 minute. This would be a killer patch to get in.

issue_title: Move the flask API to the core
issue_body:
Maintaining version constraints across packages is a nightmare and leads to issues when deploying, as described in https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-ops/issues/4.

There is no point in pretending that the web API and the core are not coupled, so let's ease maintenance by bundling the API directly with the core.

This concerns only the flask API. The legacy API will stay in this package until it is deprecated.

- [ ]  Explain the rule for windows path: use / instead of \
- [ ]  Explain that you do not need quotes even when using path with directory name containing space characters

The map methods can be greatly improved and extended. Here are some ideas:

1. Map class
  - essentially a wrapper around carto.js v4?
  - attributes such as the HTML document
  - `__repr__` is just the HTML displayed in an IPython.display.IFrame
  - output method (save to HTML)
  - possible methods:
    + add layer, remove layer
    + getter and setter methods for things like basemaps
    + update properties
    + widget properties
  - callbacks
    + e.g., plotting library filter -> change query -> change map
2. More robust:
  - Templating with Jinja

The PyPI page for the jira package (https://pypi.org/project/jira/) does not list the classifiers specified in setup.cfg, like the license and supported Python versions. I don't know how they are in setup.cfg but weren't registered during upload... Running re-register (`python setup.py register -r pypi`) may fix this.

For reference see e.g. the PyPI page for pip under "Classifiers": https://pypi.org/project/pip/

pragma solidity ^0.4.18;

contract Token {

issue_title: Where do we report bugs with Cloud Search?
issue_body: For instance, the [docs for the indexes resource](https://cloud.google.com/search/reference/rest/v1/projects/indexes) indicate that there is no way to `get` the resource for a given index ID:  the only API choice is `list`.

issue_title: Snippet in documentation is not correct.
issue_body:
# Code snippet on [readthedocs.io](readthedocs.io) page (PubSub)

- https://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pubsub/index.html#subscribing

### Provided sample

```python
def callback(message):
    print(message.data)
    message.ack()
future = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_name, callback)
```
subscribe returns a `Policy` instance not `future`. 

```python
def callback(message):
    print(message.data)
    message.ack()
policy = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_name, callback)
future = policy.future```

Is more appropriate

issue_title: maximum_billing_tier is not supported for synchronous jobs
issue_body:
This leads to inability to execute synchronous High Performance queries with this library, as the jobs will have the default maximum_billing_tier of the project (which is usually 1).

Relevant code:

```

    bucket_file = '/{}/{}'.format(bucket_name, file_name) #generally turns into something like /app_default_bucket/7jj6012fnc20250925124634jpgdl0.jpg' on the development server

gcs_file = gcs.open(bucket_file, 'w', 'image/jpeg', retry_params = write_retry_params)
out_string.seek(0) # StringIO object
gcs_file.write(out_string.getvalue())
gcs_file.close()
```

As I mentioned works perfectly when deployed. 

Something else I discovered I can get it to give me a 404 not found error if I just try to open random files.

issue_title: Change datastore-v1-protos to depend on proto-google-common-protos
issue_body:
We encountered the following issue in google-cloud-java: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-java/issues/2020

Basically, all of google-cloud-java pulls in grpc 1.2.0 but datastore-v1-protos pulls in grpc 1.0.1 through the following dependency chain:

`datastore-v1-protos:1.3.0 -> grpc-google-common-protos:0.1.0 -> grpc-all:1.0.1`

We have fixed this by overriding the version in google-cloud-java, but it would be best to fix the source.

Since 0.1.0 of grpc-google-common-protos, we have split generated packages into proto-* for message types and grpc-* for grpc stubs. Thus, the dependency you should use now is `proto-google-common-protos:0.1.9`. Then, we won't have a problem with mismatching grpc versions at all. 

Larger picture: should googleapis take over the generation of datastore-v1-protos? We have fancy batch infrastructure that can keep things up to date.
What's the plan?

issue_title: Address differences between pants.ini default interpreter constraints and target-level interpreter constraints
issue_body:
For Python interpreter selection in the v2 Pants backend, there can be differences between default interpreter constraints defined in a repo-owner controlled pants.ini and target-level constraints defined by other users. With the current selection logic, it is possible for the interpreter constraints in pants.ini to be completely ignored in favor of the user-defined, target-level interpreter constraints. 

The scope of this ticket is to devise a way to address this potential mismatch, and code up validation logic to enforce the best way to resolve this difference. 

This change would likely take place in the `setup` method of `PythonInterpreterCache` located in src/python/pants/backend/python/interpreter_cache.py. As it stands today, the `filters` variable contains target-level constraints and takes precedence over constraints from `self._python_setup.interpreter_constraints`.

These are currently not supported in PathGlobs, but will need to be to allow us to parse all BUILD files in the pantsbuild/pants repo.

They are currently banned in a few places:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/f83e2b0272e2ff1c464e453c1f74d9335e18fcc3/src/python/pants/engine/exp/fs.py#L227 is one.
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/f83e2b0272e2ff1c464e453c1f74d9335e18fcc3/src/python/pants/engine/exp/legacy/globs.py#L40-L41 is another, but it is more implicit. We drop them immediately.

----

The true test of whether this is working is whether:
```
./pants run src/python/pants/engine/exp/legacy:filemap -- src/::
```
succeeds and excludes, rather than failing with:
```
pants.build_graph.address_lookup_error.AddressLookupError: Build graph construction failed for DescendantAddresses(directory='src'):
  Failed to parse src/python/pants/reporting/BUILD:
kwargs not supported for <class 'pants.engine.exp.legacy.globs.Globs'>. Got: {'exclude': [['report.py']]}
```

issue_title: Something is amiss with resource path ordering when running tests
issue_body:
I am adding an unrelated new resource to `testprojects/resources` in https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2162/.    This is causing one of our known failing testprojects to fail in a different way than before I added the test.

To paper over the problem, I'm having to change this exclusion in ci.sh:

```
+++ b/build-support/bin/ci.sh
@@ -211,7 +211,7 @@ if [[ "${skip_testprojects:-false}" == "false" ]]; then
     # The following two targets lose out due to a resource collision, because `example_b` happens
     # to be first in the context, and test.junit mixes all classpaths.
     testprojects/maven_layout/resource_collision/example_a/src/test/java/org/pantsbuild/duplicateres/examplea:examplea
-    testprojects/maven_layout/resource_collision/example_c/src/test/java/org/pantsbuild/duplicateres/examplec:examplec
+    testprojects/maven_layout/resource_collision/example_b/src/test/java/org/pantsbuild/duplicateres/examplec:exampleb
   )
```

This was also recently changed by  @stuhood when he added a new test in this commit: 

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/commit/d2722aca13472981f2b1eb9cfb562696b8b97d63

```
commit d2722aca13472981f2b1eb9cfb562696b8b97d63
Author: Stu Hood <stuhood@gmail.com>
Date:   Sun Apr 26 17:34:15 2015 -0700

    Only add resources to the relevant target
```

I'm not sure if this commit caused a problem  - these tests really shouldn't ever be run together, at least not until we have an isolated test strategy like we have for the compile tasks.

issue_title: Literal file sets are parsed as globs in v2
issue_body:
Literal file sets end up parsed as globs: so the following are currently equivalent:
```
sources=globs('*.scala')
sources=['*.scala']
```

Parsing literal filesets as globs is not how things used to work, but it raises an interesting question: given that glob syntax supports escapes, is there any advantage to having special syntax for globs anymore?

With more and more flags being added to training, the ModelConfig object gets cluttered with parameters (and their default settings) that are often not even used as their are conditional parameters.
It would be much better to have hierarchical configs that are put together in a composite configuration. We could split configurations into
- encoder
- decoder
- attention
configurations, each of one being an individual configuration object.

Configuration objects should support JSON serialization, ideally also for nested configurations.

We might not be able to continue to use a NamedTuple for a config object as it may contain another config object as a member and this can be easily serialized.

Lets discuss if we should come up with our own ConfigObject implementation or use some existing library.

issue_title: Add user-friendly documentation for address tagging
issue_body: The current documentation isn't very helpful for quickly understanding the US Thoroughfare, Landmark, and Postal Address Data Standard. We ask users to either read [the official docs](http://www.urisa.org/clientuploads/directory/GMI/Professional%20Practice/Address%20Standard/AddressStandard_Approved_Apr11_03Class.pdf) (which is long and known to induce headaches) or [see examples in our docs](http://usaddress.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#details) (which don't cover the most confusing edge cases, and also aren't very well advertised). To encourage users to tag their own training data, we should improve the information on our documentation page and make sure it's better advertised.

issue_title: remove explanatory text in 'change language' modal
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

issues with explanatory text:

* too long
* phrase ends in a preposition
* doesn't add new information
* causing [issues with the VF release](https://github.com/fle-internal/kolibri-instant-schools-plugin/issues/148)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/33247148-b76cf734-d2d0-11e7-90c7-c98ea4efe123.png)

### Expected behavior

Copy should follow Material writing guidelines:

> * Text should be simple, concise, and direct
> * Common introductory phrases may be omitted

### User-facing consequences

More cluttered UI and extra text to read

### Context

* Kolibri version: 0.6


-----


cc @khangmach @jtamiace

Based on discussion we decided:
- Django should inject both the portion of the url it captured for routing and the remaining portion which should be routed by the js app.
- We will remove the `urls` module from plugins -- plugins should only define _one_ url, and we can entirely construct it from the existing plugin attributes for urls.

issue_title: add another decimal point to import progress
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

With very large imports on very slow connections, it's sometimes hard to tell if progress is being made.

### Expected behavior

Instead of one decimal point, we should show two.

### User-facing consequences

Users might think that progress has totally stalled, even if it hasn't.

This was observed by @jamalex 

Also relevant because of work to auto-resume imports: #3510

As a test matrix supporting several versions of cryptography has been built, we could work on this in order to support native system libraries.

Ubuntu 16.04: cryptography 1.2.3

Alternatively / supplementary: We shouldn't allow versions that aren't supported, which is part of the error in https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/2805

One suggestion would be to create a check that also takes the version into account. Current check does this:

```
>>> try:
...     from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
...     from cryptography import exceptions as crypto_exceptions
...     crypto_backend = default_backend()
...     from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa as crypto_rsa, padding as crypto_padding
...     from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization as crypto_serialization, hashes as crypto_hashes
...     CRYPTOGRAPHY_EXISTS = True
... except:
...     CRYPTOGRAPHY_EXISTS = False
```
Reports don't render properly when viewed on an iPhone (latest iOS) - navigation menus not shown.

issue_title: __init__() takes at most 4 positional arguments (6 given) raised when using GoogleServiceAccountClient
issue_body:
When you create a GoogleServiceAccountClient object, a warning is logged to stderr like so: __init__() takes at most 4 positional arguments (6 given). This is because there is a positional arguments decorator on the oauth2client.client.SignedJwtAssertionCredentials method which the GoogleServiceAccountClient calls on init.

If the __init__ decorator on the class SignedJwtAssertionCredentials is raised from 4 to 6, this warning will disappear. The other option is to name more arguments that are being passed when calling oauth2client.client.SignedJwtAssertionCredentials in the GoogleServiceAccountClient initialisation.
I'm just starting out in python and running into this error when I try to run the activate_line_items.py. I'm sure it's super basic but any help is appreciated.

What exactly is referred to as a "residual" throughout pyamg? For example [here](http://pyamg.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyamg.html#pyamg.multilevel.multilevel_solver.solve) in the `residuals` list?

I ask because I have a linear system, for which this code:
```python
A, x0, b = load_system()

residuals = []

ml = pyamg.aggregation.smoothed_aggregation_solver(A, coarse_solver="lu")
x = ml.solve(b, x0, tol=1e-8, accel="gmres", residuals=residuals)

print("residuals: ", np.array(residuals))
print("initial residual: ", np.linalg.norm(b - A*x0))
print("final residual:   ", np.linalg.norm(b - A*x))

```
gives this output:
```
residuals:  [  1.90742365e+03   2.46237095e+02   7.37640869e+01   4.24415154e+01
   2.26020896e+01   1.01097677e+01   6.54103003e+00   2.90056919e+00
   1.21443941e+00   2.94870147e-01   9.30577374e-02   3.87827062e-02
   1.21317950e-02   5.13556765e-03   1.23089031e-03   3.07927290e-04
   9.55081888e-05   2.94020487e-05   8.99521969e-06]
initial residual:  169.162191899
final residual:    7.33922070348e-07
```
Note especially, that the first item in the `residuals` array is `1.90742365e+03` vs. my custom `169.162191899`. For the final residue it's `8.99521969e-06` vs. `7.33922070348e-07`. But we have `8.99521969e-06 / 1.90742365e+03 == 4.715900261591074e-09` and `7.33922070348e-07 / 169.162191899 == 4.338570351383219e-09` which is pretty close and both are less than the specified tolerance as expected.

But with a different system, I get the following output:
```
residuals:  [  7.46798679e+03   6.12041588e+02   2.23696785e+02   4.86324879e+01
   2.12922568e+01   1.72794230e+01   1.34075286e+01   7.11332293e+00
   2.42608324e+00   1.14781268e+00   4.44119832e-01   2.42504621e-01
   1.57739497e-01   4.83938585e-02   2.04185224e-02   9.14724819e-03
   3.50229029e-03   1.03405520e-03   3.19180567e-04   1.20335344e-04
   4.69263331e-05]
initial residual:  139.669408523
final residual:    4.0189140226e-06
```
Here the ratio of the pyamg residuals is `4.69263331e-05 / 7.46798679e+03 == 6.28366578832687e-09`, which is less than the specified tolerance. But the ratio of my residuals is `4.0189140226e-06 / 139.669408523 == 2.8774475850509438e-08`, which is __greater than__ the specified tolerance, so the approximation is not good enough.

The systems are 44684 x 44684 with 442680 nonzeros and 178648 x 178648 with 1778160 nonzeros, respectively, which are still pretty small problems. Please let me know what you need to be able to track this down, I might be able to even provide the linear system data.

issue_title: Add a properties method to the AARead class
issue_body: The `AARead` class should have a `properties` method that returns a list of the values from the `dark.aa.PROPERTIES` dict, one value for each AA in the sequence. It should cache the return value in `self` so it doesn't have to be recomputed if `properties` is called more than once.

issue_title: store-metadata: mds.-1.0 ERROR: failed to authenticate: (1) Operation not permitted
issue_body:
This error has cropped up a bit lately, and has been seen by @crigor and @mdolian on users' clusters. Full logs from one cluster are as follows:

```
Jul 27 18:05:25 host1 sh[1094]: starting mds.host1 at :/0
Jul 27 18:05:25 host1 sh[1094]: 2015-07-27 18:05:25.768406 7fed4c852800 -1 auth: error parsing file /var/lib/ceph/mds/ceph-host1/keyring
Jul 27 18:05:25 host1 sh[1094]: 2015-07-27 18:05:25.789818 7fed4c852800 -1 mds.-1.0 log_to_monitors {default=true}
Jul 27 18:05:28 host1 sh[1094]: 2015-07-27 18:05:28.784466 7fed4c852800 -1 mds.-1.0 ERROR: failed to authenticate: (1) Operation not permitted
Jul 27 18:05:28 host1 sh[1094]: 2015-07-27 18:05:28.784495 7fed4c852800  1 mds.-1.0 suicide.  wanted down:dne, now up:boot
```

Note that in this instance, this didn't happen on first metadata boot, but only after the host had rebooted. I suspect a race condition in which confd has not yet run and templated a valid keyring file. Note that while we [block for confd](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/e4a54d031280d708d962a2dbde6d11e0755fc6a6/store/metadata/bin/boot#L43-L46) during initial metadata setup, we do *not* block on subsequent starts before checking to see if the keyring file exists: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/e4a54d031280d708d962a2dbde6d11e0755fc6a6/store/metadata/bin/boot#L50-L61

An example of where we do this properly is in the monitor: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/e4a54d031280d708d962a2dbde6d11e0755fc6a6/store/monitor/bin/boot#L67-L70

While this is an assertion as to the root cause here, the proposed fix should be straightforward and strictly correct, so I'll implement it.

issue_title: etcdctl fails to set keys during provisioning
issue_body: Testing showed that often on vagrant, and occasionally on EC2, the `boot` scripts of controller, builder, or database can somehow get to the `confd --onetime` section without actually having set keys or created etcd dirs with `etcd_safe_mkdir` or `etcd_set_default`. The user-visible symptom is that builder loops forever, waiting for `confd` templating, and eventually goes into `activating/auto-restart` status.

issue_title: `conda clean -p` partially deletes root Python installation on 32 bit Windows
issue_body:
There appears to be an issue with how `conda clean -p` detects whether or not a package is in use. It attempts to delete the root Python installation in the `pkgs` directory even though it is in use and then silently ignores file locking issues that come up when a file handle is held by a running process. Installing an older bugfix release and then reinstalling the latest one can be used to restore the root install.

issue_title: bedtool.tabix_intervals: Too many open files error
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm using `metaseq` (https://github.com/daler/metaseq)  and it fails with **Too many open files** error while parallel process large *.bed files. This issue is due to `bedtool.tabix_intervals()` method:

1. `metaseq` splits input in lots of chunks for parallel processing
2. each chunk is processed with `metaseq.filetype_adapters.__getitem__()` which gets intervals iterator using pybedtools
3. `bedtool.tabix_intervals()` opens `tbx` file, but never close it

I see two possible fixes:
1) Close tbx file in `tabix_intervals()`
```
def tabix_intervals(self, interval_or_string):
        #...
        tbx = pysam.TabixFile(self.fn)
        results = list(tbx.fetch(str(interval.chrom), interval.start, interval.stop))
        tbx.close()
        return BedTool(results)
```
But in this case BedTool will create intermediate tmp file for results (see #73) and `metaseq` should do not forget to clean up loads of such tmp files

2) The other way is return bedtool object and tbx file descriptor pair and let user to close tbx file after handling bedtool
```
def tabix_intervals(self, interval_or_string):
        #...

       return (BedTool(results), tbx)
```
It breaks code compatibility but doesn't create intermediate tmp files. For my own purposes I've quick fixed `pybedtools` and `metaseq` using this way

I think my both suggested ways have disadvantages and some other way should be found.

## Steps to reproduce

PLEASE DESCRIBE THE CONTEXT OF THIS BUG AND THE STEPS TO REPRODUCE!

    ret_val = func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/marcelo/.local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyqode/core/widgets/splittable_tab_widget.py", line 1284, in open_document

AssertionError:

So I have found an easy way to get an accurate physics shape for actors in blender

I have a compound root object - the armature to be animated parents to this,
each bone that is to have physics has a 'bone tag' parented to it, and a shild shape
that approximates the shape of the bone

every frame I remove parent on all the child shapes, move them to where the bone tag is, and match it's rotation,

this allows my actors to go up and down stairs by applying a forward servo motion while playing a stair walk animation.

this system works great, but when a child shape is contacting, and is moved, produces no friction,

I purpose a build in system that does all this under the hood but also computes the friction during the move maybe using substeps?

here is the system in action - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cx_9F0RNtzc
however she will not walk forward only by playing an animation - I have to combine the animation with a forward sero motion.

Okay, so 2.84 introduced API breaking changes with https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/commit/626a913866158e5956cfa2ae7f5f6350e99d783e

However, `BT_BULLET_VERSION` was already bumped up to 284 at [2.83.6](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/2.83.6/src/LinearMath/btScalar.h) (see [2.83.5](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/2.83.5/src/LinearMath/btScalar.h) where it still was 283)
To my surprise this meant [2.84](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/2.84/src/LinearMath/btScalar.h) is actually.. *drumroll* 285

- Is that intended? (It broke our codebase because we added checks for 284 because we wanted to catch ... bullet 2.84)
- How to best check for API feature level?
- How to check for a printable version number?
- Will this go back to normal?

https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/web_advanced.html#aiohttp-web-signals

signal handlers are always asynchornous now, so text is useless. Instead of this, we should write somthing like "All handlers must be asynchronous since version xxx"

issue_title: [server] Unhandled exception when None in header values
issue_body:
## Long story short

When a dictionary is passed to the `headers` kwarg (in any response, either returned or raised) and a value in this dictionary is `None`, an unhandled exception is raised that results in the client getting no response at all.

## Expected behavior

The expected behavior would be to handle it in the same way an empty string header is handled (i.e. discard it) or to catch the exception so that a 500 error can be returned.

## Actual behavior

No response arrives at the client.

## Steps to reproduce

```py
import asyncio

from aiohttp import web


def view_func(request):
    raise web.HTTPUnauthorized(headers={'a':''})


def view_raise_fail(request):
    raise web.HTTPUnauthorized(headers={'a': None})


def view_return_fail(request):
    return web.Response(status=200, headers={'a': None})


def get_app():
    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_route('GET', '/', view_func)
    app.router.add_route('GET', '/raise/fail/', view_raise_fail)
    app.router.add_route('GET', '/return/fail/', view_return_fail)
    return app


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    app = get_app()
    web.run_app(app, port=8080, host='127.0.0.1')

```

```sh
wget http://127.0.0.1:8080/
--2018-02-16 08:47:40--  http://127.0.0.1:8080/
Connecting to 127.0.0.1:8080... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 401 Unauthorized
```

```sh
wget http://127.0.0.1:8080/raise/fail/
--2018-02-16 08:48:31--  http://127.0.0.1:8080/raise/fail/
Connecting to 127.0.0.1:8080... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... No data received.
Retrying.
```

```sh
wget http://127.0.0.1:8080/return/fail/
--2018-02-16 08:48:43--  http://127.0.0.1:8080/return/fail/
Connecting to 127.0.0.1:8080... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... No data received.
Retrying.
```

The Traceback:

```py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<PATH>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/web_protocol.py", line 428, in start
    yield from resp.prepare(request)
  File "<PATH>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/web_response.py", line 327, in prepare
    return self._start(request)
  File "<PATH>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/web_response.py", line 614, in _start
    return super()._start(request)
  File "<PATH>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/web_response.py", line 394, in _start
    writer.write_headers(status_line, headers)
  File "<PATH>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/http_writer.py", line 247, in write_headers
    [k + SEP + v + END for k, v in headers.items()])
  File "<PATH>/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/http_writer.py", line 247, in <listcomp>
    [k + SEP + v + END for k, v in headers.items()])
TypeError: Can't convert 'NoneType' object to str implicitly
```


## Your environment

OS version : Linux deb9 4.9.0-3-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 4.9.30-2+deb9u5 (2017-09-19) x86_64 GNU/Linux

Python version : 3.6.4

aiohttp server version: 3.0.1

## Long story short

The `aiohttp.errors` module used to be public, documented API.
Now it's gone.

If this backwards-incompatible change is intentional, then it should be documented.

## Steps to reproduce

```console
$ python3 -c 'import aiohttp.errors'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named 'aiohttp.errors'

$ grep -Fcw aiohttp.errors CHANGES.rst HISTORY.rst 
CHANGES.rst:0
HISTORY.rst:0
```

## Your environment

aiohttp 2.0.3

Before I started working on this project, I had my own Docker client library for aiohttp;

https://github.com/tenforce/docker-py-aiohttp

But I took a very different approach. My idea was to make docker-py return futures instead of result directly. It took me a very long time before I finally figured out a solution but recently, I succeeded. I made some kind of wrapper library around docker-py that will re-use most of the code of docker-py but redirect the HTTP calls to aiohttp instead and return futures instead of directly the result. I only had to re-write the json stream parser and underlying helpers like that. At this point, the entire low-level API of docker-py is working and the high-level one will need a little bit more tweak.

Now, to be honest, I'm struggling between aiodocker and aiodockerpy. Both have pros and cons:

* For example, the code of aiodocker is clean and brand new while aiodockerpy is more hacking around the requests handlers.
* On the other hand, aiodockerpy doesn't need the same kind of maintenance: the API changes is actually handled by docker-py itself, not by aiodockerpy. I get almost everything "for free"... even the documentation is the same.

I would really like to heard your thoughts about all of this.

    def get_element(self, tag_name, attribute):
        """
            Return element in xml files which match with the tag name and the specific attribute

            :param tag_name: specify the tag name
            :type tag_name: string
            :param attribute: specify the attribute
            :type attribute: string

            :rtype: string
        """
        for i in self.xml:
            if self.xml[i] is None :
                continue
            tag = self.xml[i].getElementsByTagName(tag_name)
            if tag is None :
                return None
            for item in tag:
                value = item.getAttributeNS(NS_ANDROID_URI, attribute)

                if len(value) > 0:
                    return value
        return None
